responses.rationale: The simulated proof attempt cannot establish that all attacker-controlled authorization fields participate in cache-key material.

finding.title: Authorization cache key omits attacker-controlled scope
finding.summary: The cache key includes tenant and subject but omits scope, so two authorization contexts can share a key.

affectedLocations.reason: buildCacheKey constructs key material from tenant and subject while the scope field is attacker-controlled and authorization-relevant.

proofAttempt.prove: The implementation must show tenant, subject, and scope are all included in the cache key before authorization cache reuse.
proofAttempt.stressTest: Counterexample candidate: two requests differ only by scope and produce the same cache key.
proofAttempt.report: Keep as candidate until human security review validates exploitability and intended cache semantics.

notes: Primary positive candidate for the golden scenario.

responses.rationale: isFreshToken rejects tokens whose expiresAtEpochMs is not greater than the current time before cache lookup.
The simulated proof attempt reports a test-only helper that resembles production cache-key construction.

finding.title: Test fixture cache helper resembles production key construction
finding.summary: The candidate path is under tests/fixtures and should be rejected by scope review rather than treated as production code.

affectedLocations.reason: Fixture-only helper path is intentionally excluded from production audit scope.

proofAttempt.map: Candidate code path appears only in tests/fixtures/cache-helper.ts.
proofAttempt.prove: A production entry point would need to reach the helper for this to be actionable.
proofAttempt.stressTest: No production entry point reaches this helper in the fixture scope.
proofAttempt.report: Expected to be classified out-of-scope by the three-gate review.

notes: Negative control for the golden scenario.
